meta.title: Primary Care Practice Software | CCM, RTM & Same-Day Access | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to primary care practice operations. $13,400/month in CCM + RTM revenue. True same-day access. Annual wellness visit efficiency. Chronic care coordination.

hero.subhead: Your chronic patients qualify for CCM and RTM — $13,000+/month you're not billing. Your same-day access isn't actually accessible. Your AWVs take too long. clinIQ captures chronic care revenue, enables true same-day access, and streamlines preventive visits.

sections.content:
Primary care operates on thin margins. Visit reimbursement barely covers overhead. Patient panels grow, but revenue doesn't scale proportionally. The traditional model — more visits, faster throughput — leads to burnout.

There's a different path: capturing revenue for work that's already happening.

**Chronic Care Management (CCM):** Medicare pays $62-140/patient/month for managing patients with multiple chronic conditions. Your care coordinators already call these patients, refill their medications, and coordinate their care. CCM makes that work billable.

**Remote Therapeutic Monitoring (RTM):** Chronic disease patients already track symptoms between visits (or should). Diabetes patients track glucose. Hypertension patients check blood pressure. CHF patients track weight. RTM bills for monitoring that data.

**The math for a primary care practice:**

200 Medicare patients with 2+ chronic conditions:
- CCM at $62/patient/month (basic level) = $12,400/month
- Or CCM at $93/patient/month (complex) = $18,600/month

50 patients with RTM-appropriate conditions:
- RTM at $100/patient/month = $5,000/month

Combined opportunity: $17,000-23,000/month in revenue from work you're largely already doing.

Most primary care practices bill zero CCM and zero RTM.

CCM is Medicare's program for paying primary care to manage complex patients between visits. Patients must have 2+ chronic conditions expected to last 12+ months.

**What CCM covers:**

- Care plan development and revision
- Medication reconciliation and management
- Care coordination with specialists
- Patient/caregiver communication
- Transitional care after hospitalizations
- Referral coordination
- Lab and test result review

Your care coordinators, nurses, and physicians already do this work. CCM creates billing codes for it.

**The CPT codes:**

99490: CCM, 20+ minutes/month. ~$62.
99491: CCM by physician/QHP, 30+ minutes. ~$93.
99439: Additional 20 minutes beyond 99490. ~$47.
99487: Complex CCM, 60+ minutes. ~$140.
99489: Additional 30 minutes of complex CCM. ~$70.

**Eligibility:**

- Medicare (and some Medicaid/commercial payers)
- 2+ chronic conditions expected to last 12+ months
- Patient consent required
- Can't be billed same month as certain other care management codes

**What clinIQ does for CCM:**

Patient identification: Which patients qualify for CCM? Panel analysis identifies eligible patients.

Enrollment tracking: Consent obtained? Care plan created? Enrolled in CCM program.

Time tracking: Every qualifying interaction documented toward monthly threshold. Phone calls, messages, coordination — all captured.

Care plan management: Care plan created, updated, accessible.

Billing automation: Monthly billing generated for patients meeting criteria.

**The staffing model:**

CCM can be delivered by:
- RN care coordinators
- Licensed practical nurses
- Medical assistants (for some activities, under RN supervision)
- Physicians/APPs (at higher billing rates)

Most practices use a dedicated care coordinator model — one coordinator managing CCM for 200-400 patients.

RTM captures revenue for monitoring patient-reported data between visits. Unlike RPM (which requires physiological devices), RTM works with app-based tracking.

**Conditions appropriate for RTM in primary care:**

Diabetes: Glucose logging (fingerstick or CGM), medication compliance, symptom tracking.

Hypertension: Blood pressure logging, medication compliance.

CHF: Weight tracking, edema assessment, symptom monitoring.

COPD/Asthma: Peak flow, symptom tracking, inhaler compliance.

Osteoarthritis/Chronic pain: Pain levels, functional status, medication use.

**The CPT codes:**

98975: RTM setup — one-time. ~$20-25.
98977: RTM device supply — monthly. ~$50-60.
98980: RTM treatment management — first 20 minutes. ~$50-70.
98981: Additional 20-minute increments.

Typical: 98977 + 98980 = $100-130/month.

**RTM vs CCM:**

Can you bill both? Yes, for different activities.

- CCM bills for care coordination, care planning, communication.
- RTM bills for monitoring patient-reported data and treatment management based on that data.

The same patient can be enrolled in both if activities are distinct and documented separately.

**What clinIQ does for RTM:**

Patient app: Simple tracking for glucose, BP, weight, symptoms, medication compliance.

Condition-specific templates: Diabetes patients get different prompts than COPD patients.

Alert thresholds: Glucose >300? Weight up 5 lbs in 3 days? Alert triggers.

Automatic time tracking: Every data review documented toward threshold.

Integration with CCM: If patient has both programs, activities tracked separately, billing appropriate for each.

Every primary care practice says they offer same-day appointments. Few actually deliver.

The typical reality: Schedule filled a week ago. Same-day 'slots' gone by 9am. Acute patients told to call back tomorrow or go to urgent care.

The problem isn't demand management. It's flow visibility.

**Why same-day access fails:**

The schedule is static. It shows appointments, not reality. It doesn't show:
- Which appointments are running long
- Where gaps might open
- Which patients might no-show
- Actual provider capacity right now

Without real-time visibility, schedulers can't identify same-day capacity.

**What clinIQ does for same-day access:**

Real-time capacity: Not just scheduled appointments, but actual provider availability. 'Dr. Smith has capacity for 1 additional patient between 2-4pm.'

Running-behind visibility: Provider 20 minutes behind? Don't add same-day patients there. Provider running ahead? Add one.

No-show prediction: Patients with high no-show probability flagged. Schedulers know to wait before calling from the same-day request list.

Dynamic same-day: Instead of fixed 'same-day slots,' capacity is identified in real-time based on actual flow.

**The access math:**

'We have no same-day availability' is often false.

Typical practice: 30% of visits run shorter than scheduled. 10% of patients no-show. Providers have dead time between patients.

Aggregate: 2-4 same-day patients could fit — if you knew where to put them.

clinIQ surfaces that capacity.

Annual Wellness Visits (AWVs) are high-value for primary care. Medicare pays $179-282 for AWV. They drive quality measures and care gap closure. But they take too long.

**The AWV time problem:**

Typical AWV scheduled: 30 minutes.
Typical AWV actual: 45-60 minutes.

Why? Because AWV components aren't completed before the visit:
- Health Risk Assessment not done
- Depression screening not done
- Functional status not assessed
- Preventive services not reviewed
- Medication list not current

The physician does all of this during the visit. It takes forever.

**Pre-visit completion:**

What if patients completed Health Risk Assessment before arrival? What if depression screening (PHQ-2) was done at check-in? What if medication list was current?

The visit becomes physician review and counseling — 20 minutes instead of 45.

**What clinIQ does for AWVs:**

Pre-visit HRA: Health Risk Assessment sent electronically before appointment. Patient completes at home.

Check-in screening: PHQ-2, fall risk, functional status assessed at kiosk or tablet check-in.

Care gap visibility: Which preventive services are due? Visible before visit. Orders can be placed.

Medication reconciliation: Patient reviews medication list before visit. Discrepancies identified.

**The throughput improvement:**

With pre-visit completion: AWV takes 25 minutes instead of 45.

20 minutes saved per AWV × 5 AWVs per day = 100 minutes = 2+ additional visits.

AWV efficiency directly increases capacity.

Primary care visits vary:

- Acute visit: 15 minutes
- Chronic follow-up: 20-30 minutes
- Complex/multiple problems: 30-40 minutes
- AWV: 30-45 minutes (or 20-25 with pre-work)
- New patient: 40-60 minutes
- Procedures (IUD, biopsy, etc.): Variable

The schedule template can't capture this variation. Providers run behind. Patients wait.

**What clinIQ does for patient flow:**

Visit type awareness: Different visit types have different expected durations.

Real-time tracking: Where is each patient? How long have they been there? Who's waiting for provider?

Wait time alerts: Patient waiting 15+ minutes in room? Alert.

Provider queue: Who's ready for me? No walking to front desk to ask.

**The MA workflow:**

MAs move fast in primary care. Room patient, vitals, medication review, chief complaint, prep for provider.

clinIQ supports this:
- MA sees their queue
- Room readiness visible
- Provider notified when patient ready
- Visit-specific prep reminders (AWV needs this, acute needs that)

Primary care implementation focuses on CCM/RTM enrollment, same-day access, and patient flow.

**Week 1: Configuration**

We configure for your practice: visit types, CCM eligibility criteria, RTM conditions, same-day workflow.

Patient identification: Analysis of panel to identify CCM-eligible and RTM-appropriate patients.

**Week 2: Training**

Care coordinator training on CCM workflow. Clinical staff training on RTM. Front desk training on flow visibility. Providers trained on dashboards.

**Week 3: Go-Live**

Patient flow visibility active. CCM enrollment beginning. RTM enrollment beginning. Same-day access workflow running.

**Week 4 and Beyond**

CCM/RTM enrollment ramping. Billing starting. Flow improvements measured.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Flow visibility immediate. CCM/RTM enrollment starting.

Month 3: CCM billing at scale (100+ patients). RTM billing building. AWV efficiency improving.

Month 6: Full CCM revenue ($8,000-15,000/month). RTM contributing ($3,000-5,000/month). Same-day access functioning.

**ROI Math:**

CCM revenue: 150 patients × $75/month average = $11,250/month = $135,000/year

RTM revenue: 50 patients × $110/month = $5,500/month = $66,000/year

AWV efficiency: 2 additional visits/day × $200/visit × 250 days = $100,000/year

Total: $250,000-300,000/year in revenue and recovered capacity.

**Investment:**

Professional: $499/month

Implementation: $750 one-time

faqs.q: What's the difference between CCM and RTM?
faqs.a: CCM bills for care coordination activities — care planning, medication management, specialist coordination, patient communication. RTM bills for monitoring patient-reported data and making treatment decisions based on that data. Different codes, can both apply to same patient.
Medicare covers CCM and RTM. Some commercial payers cover CCM. RTM commercial coverage is expanding. We track payer-specific coverage to ensure appropriate billing.
Most practices use dedicated care coordinators — RNs or LPNs managing CCM for 200-400 patients each. The revenue more than covers the coordinator salary.

faqs.q: Can patients opt out of CCM/RTM?
faqs.a: Yes. CCM requires documented consent. Patients can unenroll anytime. RTM is similarly voluntary.

Real-time visibility into provider capacity. Running-behind and running-ahead visible. No-show likelihood flagged. Schedulers can identify same-day capacity dynamically rather than relying on fixed slots.

faqs.q: Can we do AWV pre-work electronically?
faqs.a: Yes. Health Risk Assessment sent before visit. Screening questions at check-in. Medication review before appointment. AWV becomes 20-minute physician review instead of 45-minute comprehensive visit.
CCM and RTM billing differs for FQHCs and RHCs. We configure based on your practice type and billing rules.

faqs.q: How fast can we scale CCM?
faqs.a: With a dedicated care coordinator: Enroll 20-30 patients/week. At scale (150+ patients) within 2-3 months.

cta.headline: See CCM/RTM revenue and same-day access

testimonial.quote: We hired a care coordinator, enrolled 180 CCM patients, and she pays for herself three times over. Plus $4K/month in RTM. Why did we wait so long?
